coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional nanomaterials and nanocomposites for sensing and monitoring of environmentally hazardous heavy metal contaminants
coredata.dc:creator: Liaquat, Hina
Imran, Muhammad
Latif, Shoomaila
Hussain, Nazim
Bilal, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: The applications of conventional sensors are limited by the long response time, high cost, large detection limit, low sensitivity, complicated usage and low selectivity. These sensors are nowadays replaced by Nanocomposite-based modalities and nanomaterials which are known for their high selectivity and physical and chemical properties. These nanosensors effectively detect heavy metal contaminants in the environment as the discharge of heavy metals into natural water as a result of human activity has become a global epidemic. Exposure to these toxic metals might induce many health-related complications, including kidney failure, brain injury, immune disorders, muscle paleness, cardiac damage, nervous system impairment and limb paralysis. Therefore, designing and developing novel sensing systems for the detection and recognition of these harmful metals in various environmental matrices, particularly water, is of extremely important. Emerging nanotechnological approaches in the past two decades have played a key role in overcoming environmentally-related problems. Nanomaterial-based fabrication of chemical nanosensors has widely been applied as a powerful analytical tool for sensing heavy metals. Portability, high sensitivity, on-site detection capability, better device performance and selectivity are all advantages of these nanosensors. The detection and selectivity have been improved using molecular recognition probes for selective binding on different nanostructures. This study aims to evaluate the sensing properties of various nanomaterials such as metal-organic frameworks, fluorescent materials, metal-based nanoparticles, carbon-based nanomaterials and quantum dots and graphene-based nanomaterials and quantum dots for heavy metal ions recognition. All these nano-architectures are frequently served as effective fluorescence probes to directly (or by modification with some large or small biomolecules) sense heavy metal ions for improved selectivity. However, efforts are still needed for the simultaneous designing of multiple metal ion-based detection systems, exclusively in colorimetric or optical fluorescence nanosensors for heavy metal cations.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional nanomaterials and nanocomposites for sensing and monitoring of environmentally hazardous heavy metal contaminants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The applications of conventional sensors are limited by the long response time, high cost, large detection limit, low sensitivity, complicated usage and low selectivity. These sensors are nowadays replaced by Nanocomposite-based modalities and nanomaterials which are known for their high selectivity and physical and chemical properties. These nanosensors effectively detect heavy metal contaminants in the environment as the discharge of heavy metals into natural water as a result of human activity has become a global epidemic. Exposure to these toxic metals might induce many health-related complications, including kidney failure, brain injury, immune disorders, muscle paleness, cardiac damage, nervous system impairment and limb paralysis. Therefore, designing and developing novel sensing systems for the detection and recognition of these harmful metals in various environmental matrices, particularly water, is of extremely important. Emerging nanotechnological approaches in the past two decades have played a key role in overcoming environmentally-related problems. Nanomaterial-based fabrication of chemical nanosensors has widely been applied as a powerful analytical tool for sensing heavy metals. Portability, high sensitivity, on-site detection capability, better device performance and selectivity are all advantages of these nanosensors. The detection and selectivity have been improved using molecular recognition probes for selective binding on different nanostructures. This study aims to evaluate the sensing properties of various nanomaterials such as metal-organic frameworks, fluorescent materials, metal-based nanoparticles, carbon-based nanomaterials and quantum dots and graphene-based nanomaterials and quantum dots for heavy metal ions recognition. All these nano-architectures are frequently served as effective fluorescence probes to directly (or by modification with some large or small biomolecules) sense heavy metal ions for improved selectivity. However, efforts are still needed for the simultaneous designing of multiple metal ion-based detection systems, exclusively in colorimetric or optical fluorescence nanosensors for heavy metal cations.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are the elements that occur naturally with a high atomic mass and density of more than 5 gcm−3. Transition metals, post-transition metals and lanthanides from periods 4–6 and groups 13–16 are the heavy metals (Koller and Saleh, 2018). The term heavy metal is employed for the substance with potential toxicity and is loosely used to refer to both metals and metalloids. Heavy metal is a vague term and has been defined differently based on density, atomic mass, atomic number, etc., by many authors (Chandra et al., 2022). Each living organism needs certain number of heavy metals like copper, cobalt, Iron, zinc, molybdenum, and manganese (Roshan and DeVries, 2021) but their higher concentration is toxic. The term heavy metal is being replaced by metal trace elements as heavy metals are less toxic and required for the normal functioning of living organisms. Excessive intake of these heavy metals can be detrimental to the living organism. Some of the heavy metals, for example, lead, methyl mercury, nickel-cadmium and copper, are toxic when present in very low amounts in amphibians, water species and birds. Mercury, chromium, cadmium, nickel and lead beyond their threshold cause nervous system, respiratory, kidney, skin and liver disorders in human beings (Sall et al., 2020). The concentration of metals in soil can be as low as 1 mg/kg, or it can be as high as 100,000 mg/kg (Singh et al., 2011).

The mainland or water contaminant heavy metals that cause considerable damage to the biosphere are present in municipal wastes, incinerate, sludge/municipal compost, exudates, pesticides, metalliferous mines residues, fertilizers and all the smelting industries (Nachana’ a Timothy, 2019). Few heavy metals are crucial to life on earth in limited amounts, and when the intake of these metals increases in the body, they can cause adverse effects. Heavy metals are introduced into the environment through many anthropogenic and natural processes. Waste produced from industrial activities like metal smelting, chemical industries, manufacturing processes and electroplating is released into the environment without treatment. This waste contains high concentrations of heavy metals (Li et al., 2019a; Zwolak et al., 2019). Toxic heavy metals are discharged in the deltaic and floodplain regions by subsurface contamination (Chakraborti et al., 2015; Karim, 2000). Aggregation of heavy metal ions in underwater deposits can be a re-suspension water source (Wu et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015). The different heavy metals with their applications and hazards within different permissible limits are enumerated in Table 1 .

Drinking water is reported to be contaminated with various heavy metals. Main drinking water sources commonly fall into three categories: surface water, desalinating seawater (mostly in fresh water-scarce regions) and groundwater (Kim et al., 2015). Drinking water is also comprised of spring water, harvested rainwater, and bottled water (Sullivan and Leavey, 2011). Persistent discharge of heavy metal contents beyond their threshold levels in the environment causes serious toxicity to life by contaminating food chains. Aquatic life is disturbed adversely by the bioaccumulation of these heavy metals in riverine ecosystems. Heavy metals are potential neurotoxins to fish. They hinder the interaction of fish with its environment by combining with the chemical stimuli produced in fish. The accumulation of heavy metals in fish can cause damage to human beings on consumption as food (Ali et al., 2019; Fatima et al., 2020). Heavy metals are well-thought-out to be the key pollutants because they cannot be degraded while other organic pollutants can be broken down into other less-lethal compounds (Sankhla et al., 2016a, 2016b). The dangerous and carcinogenic effects of heavy metals have always drawn the attention of scientists and toxicologists to detect them and explore new methods to remove them. Atomic absorption (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma mass (ICP-MS) spectroscopies are the classic analysis techniques utilized for the recognition of heavy metals (Pohl, 2009; Silva et al., 2009). Many drawbacks are linked with these techniques, such as time consumption, expensive and complex instrumentation. Voluminous research has approved to minimize the use of these techniques and replace them with fast, simple utilization and cost-effective means for detecting heavy metals. Electrochemical methods have also been developed to overcome the shortcomings of such techniques. They offer many benefits which are related to simplification of the instrument, in-field application, and cost-effectiveness. Most commonly used electrochemical methods include potentiometric and voltametric techniques (Xie and Bakker, 2015; Privett et al., 2008). Voltametric technique uses initial zero-current perturbation by either applying time-varying potential, while in potentiometric technique, the sample composition is determined by potential changes on the two electrodes (Wang, 2007). Potentiometric sensors are divided into two categories. One of which is field-effect transistors (FETs), while the other is ion-selective electrodes (ISEs). Many ion-selective transistors have been advanced to spot trace concentrations of toxic heavy metal ions (Bobacka et al., 2008; Merkoci and Alegret, 2007). Many electrochemical sensors made of nanomaterials have also been created for signal enhancement (Aragay et al., 2011a; D ü zg ü n et al., 2011). For further clarity, instrument-based sensors have been classified as follows (Fig. 1 ).

Conventional materials used in electrochemical sensors are being replaced now a days with nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes, graphene, and metal nanoparticles) and luminescent metal-organic frameworks (Fig. 2 ) (Li et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2010; Zhang and Fang, 2010a). Nanocomposite based nanomaterials have gotten the attention of human beings due to their amazing selectivity, stability, sensitivity, processability and repeatability. These properties have rendered nanocomposites with effective analytical performance against food contaminants and environmental pollutants (Anh et al., 2021). Modification in nanomaterials with various effective inorganic and organic materials has been made to get the required properties. Large surface area, microporosity, scalable synthesis, excellent chemical stability, crystallinity and substantial biocompatibility of metal-organic frameworks enhance electroanalytical sensing properties, catalytic activity and electrical conductivity (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022a). The enhanced selectivity and sensitivity of a nanosensor is originated from its size-dependent property, high functionality, large surface-to-volume ration, high reactivity, organic ligand anchorage and covalent functionalization (Borah et al., 2015). Sensitive and robust nanosensors based on organic and inorganic nanomaterials have been developed worldwide for environmental sensing of transition metal ions and heavy metal ions (Khachatryan and Khachatryan, 2019). The nanomaterials used in chemical sensors have significantly improved their detection limit and reproducibility for selective identification of the metal ions (Kaur et al., 2015).

Fluorescent chemosensors are typically made from 3 components, an ionophore (receptor), a spacer (the linker) and a fluorophore (the signaling moiety) (Fig. 3 ) (De Silva et al., 1997; Czarnik J.P., 1997). The ionophore part carries the process for cation-recognition by changing fluorophore signal due to agitation of different processes (Valeur and Leray, 2000; Shamsipur et al., 2006; Aragoni et al., 2007). The most common types of these sensors are electronic energy transfer (EET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET).

In case of PET sensing process, fluorescence is appeared and quenched at times. H+ or M+ binds the receptor causing fluorescence and detachment of these ions from receptor quench the fluorescence. This indicates that the detachment of (fluorophore) H+ or M+ with the receptor is called fluorescence “switch off” and attachment is defined to be as fluorescence “switch on”. Concentration of metal and nature of cation and receptor decides the optical transmission. A turn-on sensor is the one which activates fluorescence by PET process (Ramachandran and Samanta, 1997). Fluorescence is extinguished by the detachment process and the sensor thus resulted is named turn-off sensor. Fluorescence quenching is a result of the pervasive nature of many toxic-metal cations. The most wanted scheme of an ISE sensor utilizes fluorescence enhancement. The process of fluorescence quenching is not involved in EET. Direct orbitals overlap of fluorophore with the cations takes place by the aid of energy transfer mechanism. This is denoted to be Dexter energy-transfer phenomenon (Lakowicz, 1999; Turro, 1991; Guillet, 1985). Energy transfer that occurs without any direct orbital overlap, on the other hand, is termed as Forster energy transfer phenomenon (Fan et al., 2009). The two mechanisms are different owing to variable distance in donor and acceptor (Dutta and Das, 2012; De Acha et al., 2019).

The general working principle of fluorescent sensor involves light emission by a fluorophore which is excited by radiations at lower wavelengths (Lakowicz, 2006; Elosua et al., 2014). Concentration of target analyte decides the lifetime and intensity of light emission (Caselli, 2015). Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), porphyrins, fluorescent aptamers, organic dyes, quantum dots, or DNAzymes are being reported to screen heavy metals in drinking water (Yang et al., 2016; He et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2011; Cai et al., 2013). Small detection limits, short time response and amazing sensitivity has drawn the attention of scientists to synthesize fluorescent sensors (Ahmed et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2015; Jiang and Pu, 2017; Li et al., 2017). Some notable and reported possible detection mechanisms are photoinduced electron transfer, intramolecular proton transfer, intramolecular charge transfer, enhanced chelation fluorescence and fluorescence resonance-energy transfer (Wu et al., 2011; Xu et al., 2010; Manna et al., 2018; Taya et al., 2018; Venkatesan et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2019; Huo et al., 2017).

Luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) are a new robust substitute among many fluorescent sensors and are composed of a combination of metal ions with linkers and have competently been used to improve problems regarding food safety monitoring (Zhang et al., 2018). Due to unique MOFs building blocks, fluorescence phenomenon can be initiated both from ligands and metal centers (Fang et al., 2018). The phenomenon of luminescence can initiate from any part of the LMOFs when a guest specie approaches it, as shown in Fig. 4 (Samanta et al., 2020).

Fluorescence is most commonly observed in rigid planar structure, electron-donating substituents and extended conjugated double bond system containing substances. Mostly LMOFs are constructed by using organic ligands with defined π-conjugated system (Du et al., 2021; Allendorf et al., 2009). The free rotation and vibration of the ligands can be easily reduced by MOFs containing rigid skeletons. The light-emitting organic groups in MOFs can thus be successfully excited to generate fluorescence (Fard et al., 2016; Jin et al., 2021; Nagarkar et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2015). Sensitivity is important but it is believed that selectivity is possibly the most fundamental premise to develop any sensor. Molecular sieving effect has been well reported to be the reliable method for achieving MOFs selectivity. Response time of a sensory material with selectivity is also another important feature which is clearly inspired by nature, shape, and size of porous MOFs. MOFs are considered better than other types of materials in the majority of parameters like; hydrolytic stability, the material's stability and particularly its chemical inertness (Gelfand and Shimizu, 2016; Howarth et al., 2016; ul Qadir et al., 2015; Burtch et al., 2014). An important issue for MOFs seeking applicability as real-time gas or vapors sensors is the ability for most of the MOFs related to water adsorption. Moisture can significantly reduce response time and sensitivity, as well as compromise the detection. In recent years, several notable reviews and reports have emerged which demonstrate the applications of MOFs in solid-state sensors as devices (Stavila et al., 2014).

Versatile structures, morphologies and distinct advantages of various MOF structures have made them excellent luminescent materials to sense various metal cations like Cr3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and many others (Zhao et al., 2016a; Xu et al., 2016; Liu and Yan, 2016; Wang et al., 2016). This reflects the increasing trend of using MOFs to detect and sense an extensive range of metal ions. According to the literature, interaction-based sensing in nature is relatively fast. For instance, Lewis basic pyridyl functionalized luminescent MOFs have been reported for Fe3+ and Zr4+ (Lewis’s acids) recognition through Lewis’s acid–base interaction (Liu et al., 2017a; Chen et al., 2018a; Barkhordarian and Kepert, 2017; Wang et al., 2017a). A porphyrin ring (H2-TCPP) tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin has been reported to be a building block of Tb-TCPP (terbium-tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin) to achieve “turn-on” M3+ luminescent probes (Fu et al., 2021). The fluorescence of Tb-TCPP increased immediately when it interacted with Cr3+, Al3+ and Fe3+ ions, as shown in Fig. 5 . The occurrence of additional metal cations, on the other hand, does not give a substantial fluorescence response to Tb-TCPP. Tb-TCPP has a detection limit for M3+ ions in the nano Molar range, which is lesser than the detection limit of other MOF sensors (Li et al., 2019b).

Lanthanide MOFs have collected vast attention as chemical senser on account of their striking luminescence properties like large Stokes shifts, strong spin orbit coupling constant, long-lived emission, naturally sharp line emission and large luminescence quantum yield (Dang et al., 2012; de Lill et al., 2009; Zhu et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2007; Saraci et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2019). By virtue of these characteristics, many Ln-MOFs have been established as chemosensors to detect toxic gases, small molecules, heavy metal ions, and so on (Sun et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2018; Islamoglu et al., 2020). An Ytterbium-based MOF structure (ZJU-27) has been reported as new electrochemical sensor for Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneous detection (Ye et al., 2020a) by employing square wave voltammetry (SWV) procedure. This MOF is improved by boosted sensitivity with increased contact area and bigger selectivity via the size exclusion impact. It has been observed that pore size reduction may occur by large radius of lanthanide ion centers thus leading to more microporous structures (Jiang et al., 2010) and aid in the accumulation of small metal ions on the surface of electrode. A number of microporous Ln-MOFs are utilized to pre-concentrate and selectively adsorb Pb2+ ions on water samples (Jamali et al., 2016).

There is enormous development in the field of nanomaterial’s research especially in the past few decades (Zhang et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016). There are several renowned nanomaterials which improve the rate of electron transfer. Among these nanospheres, nanorods and nanowires are more widely recognized. Various nanomaterials also have been synthesized and operated for detecting heavy metal ions. Electrochemical sensors are improved with diverse nanomaterials and exhibited unique properties like greater selectivity and sensitivity (Wang et al., 2013a). The sensors designed with appropriate interface helps in identification of trace heavy metal cations from any type of sample by efficient and rapid means (Abdelmnim et al., 2019). Colorimetric methods showed a change in color on aggregation of nanoparticles especially for silver NPs and gold NPs as shown in Fig. 6 (Du et al., 2013; Sung and Wu, 2014; Chansuvarn et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2014).

Han and his coworkers reported the detection mercury ion by the advancement of organic molecules-based sensors (Guo et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2017b). Gumpu et al., described a review on electrochemical method to detect and describe toxicity mechanisms of numerous heavy metals (Gumpu et al., 2015). Barton et al., on the other hand, elaborated the importance of some screen-printed electrodes for in detecting toxic metal ions (Barton et al., 2016). Electrochemical sensors are widely used detection tools for environmental pollutants these days. Nanomaterials based electrocatalysts are used to enhance analytical performance and amend the electrodes of electrochemical sensors. One such electrocatalyst is Co3O4 nanoparticles which possess outstanding electrochemical, optical, electrocatalytic and electrical properties (Khand et al., 2021). The electrochemical biosensors based on bulk materials have low selectivity. This problem is resolved using a conducting nanocomposite material of carbon nanotubes combined with metal nanoparticles and organic species in the electrochemical sensor. These electrochemical sensors based on nanocomposites are effective against many analytes such as heavy metal ions, organic moieties, biomolecules and pesticide residuals (Hussain et al., 2022). Colorimetric sensors have gained popularity due to their simplicity, ease of measurement, and large sensitivity (Zhang and Fang, 2010b; Thatai et al., 2014; Aragay et al., 2011b). A surfactant (CTAB) Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide has been directed for the synthesis of Au nanorods that are outstanding nanosensors to detect Fe3+ions present in water with LOD of 100 ppb for Fe3+ions in a time of 10 min (Kauffman et al., 2010a).

The field of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) has drawn grand interest because of their applications in fuel cells, advanced electronics, sensors and biotechnology/medicine (Kauffman et al., 2010b). Coupling of metal nanoparticles with CNTs has brought a keen interest in the coupled NP-CNT systems exhibiting enhanced sensitivity (Marichy et al., 2012). These modifications in CNTs by nanomaterials have proved to be crucial in the screen-printed electrochemical (SPEs) sensors’ performance as given in Fig. 7 (Ping et al., 2012; Ruecha et al., 2015). CNTs have been sorted into two classes MWCNTs (multi-walled CNTs) and SWCNTs (single-walled CNTs) with some excellent properties like, chemical stability, wide electrochemical window, high conductivity, and huge surface area which make them promising materials to modify electrodes (Iijima and Ichihashi, 1993; Sarkar and Srinives, 2018). Till this day, CNTs have gained huge popularity in developing electrochemical sensors with high capacity.

It is well established in literature that screen-printed sensor modified with SWCNTs could act as an effective platform for electrochemical heavy metal ions detection (Luo et al., 2018). Molinero-Abad et al., established an enabled disposable sensor of SWCNTs to strip detect Pb2+ ions with 72.128 nA nM−1 sensitivity by utilizing (DPASV) differential-pulse-anodic-stripping voltammetry (Molinero-Abad et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017c). The fabrication of this sensor was carried out by drop-casting method. SWCNTs solution was first drop-casted on the surface of carbon electrode followed by gold nanoparticles electrodeposition on the SPCE/SWCNTs with aid of HAuCl4 solution. Injang et al., fabricated a disposable MWCNTs doped screen-printed-carbon nanotube-electrode (SPCNTE) to determine Cd2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ ions simultaneously (Injang et al., 2010). The sensitivity of electrochemical sensor was improved by electroplating bismuth fluoride film on the surface of electrode to get a nanocomposite SPCNTE/BiF. Under improved conditions, the said sensor showed 2.0–100.0 μg/L, 2.0–100.0 μg/L and 12.0–100.0 μg/L linear range for lead, cadmium, and zinc ion respectively. Detection limit (LOD) was 0.2 μg/L for Pb2+, 0.8 μg/L for Cd2+ and for Zn2+ it is 11.0 μg/L (Wang et al., 2017b). The unique properties like broad surface area, unparalleled biocompatibility, simple organic functionality and decrease in overpotentials of carbon-based nanocomposites are accredited to the formation of charge-transfer complexes and acceptable conductivity because of the presence of sp2 hybridized layers (Mehmandoust et al., 2021; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022b). Metal and metal oxide nano particles with carbon nanomaterials have strong mediator potential and hence, can be used to manufacture electrochemical sensors with high sensitivity (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022c).

The CQDs are described typically as zero-dimensional nanoparticles of carbon with size lower than 10 nm and a crystal parameter of 0.34 attributed to a d spacing of about (002) for the graphite. Different functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and epoxy on CQDs attribute to their solubility in aqueous media and easily functionalize by offering a platform with a variety of species (Yuan et al., 2016; Molaei, 2019a). The amount of oxygen in the oxidized CQDs varies from 5 to 50 wt% depending on the synthetic route (Lim et al., 2015). CQDs have been the center of attraction in sensors with small LOD in picomolar, nanomolar and even femtomolar scales (Chen et al., 2018b; Liu et al., 2013a). Different advantages like easy functionalization, ease of synthesis, high quantum yield, chemical stability, excellent brightness etc. Make CQDs good candidates for their applications in sensor (Molaei, 2020). Sensors that contain CQDs generally involves the prevention of the quenching or quenching phenomenon in fluorescent emission in CQDs when metal ions like Hg2+ is present (Molaei, 2019b). The quenching phenomenon in CQDs are usually static quenching, dynamic quenching, PET, fluorescence-resonance-energy transfer (FRET) and Inner filter effect-based fluorescent sensing systems (IFE) as shown in Fig. 8 .

Static quenching happens through an interaction amongst CQDs, and the quencher thus led to develop non-fluorescent ground-state complex. In dynamic quenching, CQDs returns to ground level from the excited state through a collision in quencher and CQDs through energy or charge transfer (Zu et al., 2017). In FRET pairs, the energy transfer interaction takes place between 10 nm apart two fluorescent components. The transfer occurs from a higher energy donor to a lower energy acceptor to enhance fluorescence (Probst et al., 2013). The IFE happens by absorbing the emission or excitation light through an absorber in the sensor (Chen et al., 2018c). The CQDs was synthesized via hydrothermal method and from Jinhua bergamot plant with a quantitative yield of 50.78%. These CQDs were utilized to selectively detect Fe3+ and Hg2+ among other metal ions like Fe2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ag+ and Ca2+ with an exceptional detection limit of 0.075 μM and 5.5 nM for Fe3+ and Hg2+ (Yu et al., 2015).

The chemical sensor with application based upon colorimetric detection of metal ions is more sensitive and way too simple than conservative methods. It eradicates the demand of classy instruments as the results can be perceived by “naked-eye” (Sedghi et al., 2015). Among different nanomaterials, AuNPs have been extensively investigated to be used in colorimetric sensors as shown in Fig. 9 (Yue et al., 2016). Guo et al. utilized the non-modified gold NPs to sense Cd2+ in a rice grain without any markers for first trial. The basic process involves the dispersion of AuNPs without amending and the presence of Cd2+ accumulate gold nanoparticles, however other cations cannot do it in similar condition (Guo et al., 2014). Kumar et al. produced core-shell SiO2–Au nanocomposites and applied them as colorimetric sensor in water sample for cadmium ions detection. In this research, SiO2–Au nanocomposites presented higher sensitivity towards Cadmium ion, that nanocomposite had twenty times more sensitivity than simple AuNPs (Thatai et al., 2015). Wu et al. recently reported an ultrasensitive colorimetric route to synthesize AgNPs modified with 5-sulfosalicylic acid (SAA-AgNPs) for Cd2+ sensing. This affordable approach not merely showed brilliant selectivity for Cd2+ within 2 min, but also had high reproducibility to sense Cd2+ in actual samples with high recovery range of 93%–110% (Jin et al., 2015). It can be concluded from the literature available that by utilizing the fluorescent behavior of few metal nanoclusters, carbon dots, organic dyes (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022a–c), QDs and fluorescent polymers, various colorimetric sensors can be fabricated with on/off analyte-induced fluorescent behavior (Liu et al., 2020).

Advancement of fluorescent sensors is founded typically on alterations induced by analyte in physicochemical properties of the fluorescent chemosensor or fluorophore. Anisotropy, intensity of fluorescence and lifetime are the properties which determine physicochemical nature of a fluorophore. Fluorescent constituent sends signals for the binding event as well as for receptor component to specifically bind to the analyte in fluorescence nanosensor (Lichtman and Conchello, 2005). Many nanocomposite-based fluorescence sensors are synthesized. Au-NPs/rGO nanocomposite is used as a fluorescence sensor in Ramen spectroscopy to detect lead ions by causing a shift, as presented in Fig. 10 (Zhao et al., 2016b). Fluorescent sensors have two key characters: a chelating moiety for metal with no less than one fluorophore competent of light absorption and emission. It is necessary to do job as a sensor that metal binding must modify one of the structures (molecular or electronic) of the fluorescent sensor. Any Change in electronic structure of the sensor may cause an alteration in the wavelength or intensity of emission or absorption of light, while alterations in molecular structure may change the orientation or distance within donor–acceptor pair of fluorophores. A fluorescence-based microscope allows imagining of alterations in the fluorescence and ultimately a required metal ion can be probed or detected in a resolved manner (Carter et al., 2014).

The fluorescence-based sensing is typically based upon quenching mechanism by effective charge transfer mechanism. Fluorescence quenching is quite common when fluorophore receptor systems are bound to transition metal ions (Guo et al., 2004). Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions owing to the paramagnetic nature have frequently very efficient fluorescent quenchers. It is hard to advance “turn on” fluorescent probes for these ions (Chen et al., 2013). Complexation with such metal ion is remarkably known to boost fluorescence signal by dropping the charge transmission interactions (Prodi et al., 2000). This kind of sensor is based on the FRET mechanism, which depends on the fluorophore, quenching materials, and fluorescence quenching (Zuo et al., 2016). The literature reveals that up till now, numerous nanomaterials (for example, carbon nitride, graphene oxide, CNTs and metal sulfide nanosheets) are being functionalized to fluorescent sensing of metal ions (Zhang et al., 2015a; Wang et al., 2013b; Mao et al., 2015; Niu et al., 2010).

The selection criterion for a fluorescent probe is one of the most crucial factors that helps to determine the quality and type of information obtained. This selection warrants a special attention. Various types of fluorescent probes have been reported and mainly categorized as solvato-chromic probes, pH-sensitive probes anisotropy probes, rigido-chromic probes etc. (Keeling-Tucker and Brennan, 2001). The fluorescent probes are incorporated into the materials when the entrapped molecules and hydrolyzed precursors are combined and permitted to gel during the sol-gel process. After that when condensation takes place, these probes get entrapped within the available pores during the formation of the material and eventually become the part of this matrix network. The same has been outlined in detail in a review reported by Dunn and Zink (1997). It is described as follow, the probe may occur in different environments within matrix network: adsorbed on pore wall or embarrassed within a network between pores or the liquid filled pore. The probe can combine with either the inorganic (silica) or organic subphase in case of nanocomposites. Therefore, the choice of a specific probe to examine the internal environment has great importance because different probes will account for different locations.

Quantum dots (QDs) also referred as 0D nanomaterials have attracted lot of attention for detection of heavy metals (Chen et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2014a). Compared with traditional probes such as lanthanide probes and organic dyes, quantum dots (QDs) possess supreme characteristics like tunable wavelength, big Stokes’s shift and broad absorption spectra etc. Providing novel method to quantify heavy metals (Gao et al., 2005; Smith et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2016a; Michalet et al., 2005). Various functionalized quantum dots have been reported so far to selectively recognize ions such as copper, silver, mercury, and cadmium (Liang et al., 2004; Mahmoud, 2012; Ruedas-Rama et al., 2007; Touceda-Varela et al., 2008; Chen et al., 2008; Dong et al., 2006; Li et al., 2007). Liu and his co-workers produced CdTe/CdS QDs modified with glyphosate which proved to be an effective probe for Cu2+ with short time response of about 10 min (Liu et al., 2012). Ding et al. used l-glutathione to cap ZnSe QDs for selective identification of copper ions with little LOD of 0.2 nM (Ding et al., 2014).

Among other materials, Graphene is also a familiar carbon-based material and is generally synthesized by mechanically exfoliating the graphite. Graphene oxide (GO) includes phenol hydroxyl, epoxide and carboxylic groups and is often produced by the well-known Hummers' method. It is established in the literature that embedding heteroatoms (e.g., P, B and N) in carbon network of the graphene provides more active sites and eventually enhances the surface hydrophilicity. Graphene and its derivatives are prone to surface treatment. They are considered good candidates for good hostage to immobilize other nanoparticles. This property of these particles has been applied to advance the sensitivity and intensify signals of toxic metal ion sensors, as given in Fig. 11 (Liu et al., 2013b).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have got enormous attraction in recent years with vast applications in sensor, biological imaging, photovoltaic devices, and phototransistors (Ponomarenko et al., 2008; Ritter and Lyding, 2009; Li et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2015a; Sun et al., 2013a; Konstantatos et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2012). Advancement in conservative fluorescence probes (inorganic semiconductor QDs and organic dyes) has been stalled from certain drawbacks such as short lifetime, poor photostability and easy photobleaching. GQDs exhibited excessive benefits due to their excellent biocompatibility, high photoluminescence, stable emission, and good resistance to photo-bleaching (Qian et al., 2014; Ran et al., 2013a; Al-Ogaidi et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2011). Due to these characteristics, GQDs have been reported to be excellent alternatives for fluorescence sensor both in heavy metal detection and biological system (Niu et al., 2018a). It is also reported that nanoparticles of Au, Pd and Ag own peroxidase imitative activity. Their combination with graphene enhanced their stability and catalytic performance. In this analogy, a series of heavy metal ion sensors have been proposed (Chen et al., 2015). GO or Graphene is a substrate to stack fluorophore for straight with fluorescent nanoparticles or indirectly by means of biomolecules (Liu et al., 2017d). Liu et al. fabricated Ag–In–Zn–S QDs with GO and used them for subsequent fluorescence quenching to detect Cu2+ (Liu et al., 2016b).

GQDs have many organic groups on the surface and exhibit strong fluorescence. These can be employed as metal ion fluorescence sensing probes. Several metal cations including Au3+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr6+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ag+ have been identified using different types of fluorescent sensors based on GQD (Xu et al., 2015a, 2015b; Shi et al., 2015b; Sun et al., 2013b; Huang et al., 2013, 2015; Zhang et al., 2015b; Qi et al., 2013a; Suryawanshi et al., 2014a; Yang et al., 2015). Majority of these studies have been conducted on Cu2+, Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions because of their high toxicity and prominent role in biological systems. Lu's et al., described that Hg2+ presence can stimulate the peroxidase imitative action of nanohybrids of rGO/PEI/Pd thereby encouraging the color change and effective oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine in solvent, making sensitive and selective identification of mercury ion with 0.39 nM LOD (Zhang et al., 2017).

Zhou et al. reported fluorescent probe of pure GQDs having green fluorescence to detect Fe3+ with an LOD of 5 nM. The LOD was inferior to previously reported Fe3+ probes which contain rhodamine 6G derivatives, 8-quinolinol, fluorescent nanographene and quinazolinone derivatives (Zhou et al., 2013). Li et al. reported electrochemical synthesis of a fluorescent S-GQD probe having blue-green fluorescence and observed extra sensitive Fe3+ fluorescence response than pure GQDs. The more sensitive response of S-GQD could be attributed to S doping that might adjusted the electronic density of graphene QDs which promotes coordination contact between hydroxyl group of phenol and Fe3+ on Sulphur containing GQDs (S-GQDs) surfaces (Li et al., 2014). Chakraborti et al. used pure fluorescent GQDs probe for Hg2+ sensing in aqueous solution. High selectivity of probe for Hg2+ was observed by comparison of the quenched fluorescence intensity of GQD solution with that of different metal ions (Na+, Li+, Ca2+, K+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) at same concentration (Chakraborti et al., 2013). Wang et al. described GQDs like a fluorescent probe having blue fluorescence for Cu2+ ions recognition. The sensor was sensitive towards Cu2+ in comparison to other cations (Wang et al., 2014). Qian et al. reported a highly efficient fluorescent aptamer-functionalized GQDs sensor for Pb2+ ions. The fluorescent probe of aptamer-functionalized GQDs were fabricated and then incorporated on the GO surface. The aptamer/GQD/GO system was used for Pb2+ ions quantification. The aptamer-GQDs liberation from GO was triggered by adding Pb2+ to recover fluorescence (Qian et al., 2015).

Ligand modified with other reagents has also been used to enhance the sensing properties of nanosensors. Thymine-functionalized GQDs may also be operated to detect Hg2+ as fluorescent probes. Achadu with his team synthesized zinc phthalocyanine modified with thymine and thymine modification on GQDs probes for specific detection of Hg2+ (Achadu and Nyokong, 2017). Shi's team fabricated GQDs put to work with 3,9-dithia-6-monoazaundecane (DMA) to encourage the development of DMA-GQD-Pb2+-tryptophan by coordination and electrostatic relation between Pb2+ and the ligands because of the strong ET interactions (Qi et al., 2013b).

Qu's group reported the fabrication of amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs) with a large quantitative yield (QY) via hydrothermal reaction of GQDs in ammonia. The said af-GQDs demonstrated Cu2+ detection with a much higher selectivity than greenish-yellow fluorescent-GQDs (g-GQDs) (Sun et al., 2013c). Suryawanshi et al. stated the production of GQDs from debris biomass and carried out their modification by terminating amine modified GQDs (Am-GQDs). In addition to Ag+, there are many metal ions that could reduce fluorescence in the same way for Am-GQDs. The addition of cysteine renewed fluorescence only for Ag+ (Suryawanshi et al., 2014b). Baruah et al. reported cross-linking of amine-functionalized GQDs into polyvinyl acetate (PVA), resulting in hybrid hydrogels. These hydrogels rejoined to Cu2+, Co2+, and Fe2+ with different coloration and provided a straightforward, sensitive, easy and effective method to optically sense metal ions in aqueous media (Baruah and Chowdhury, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly with other nanoparticles for sensing
ce:section.ce:para: It is a well-established fact that graphene can anchor different nanoclusters and metal nanoparticles. The resulting hybridized assembly offered great stability. Keeping an eye on this stability, some researchers attempted to produce metal nanoparticles embellished GQDs and then examined the impact of this decoration on the process of fluorescence of GQDs. Ran et al., attached Ag+ on the surface of GQDs with help of electrostatic contact and observed the quenching in fluorescence of GQDs owing to GQDs/AgNPs hybrids. Addition of biothiols boost quenching of fluorescence in GQDs. This is because of the reductant properties of biothiols which help bridging AgNPs with each other. The highly sensitive as well as selective detection of biothiols and Ag+ is realized by using AgNP-embellished GQDs (Ran et al., 2013b). Niu et al. decorated GQDs with Gold NPs by hybridized DNAs modification. This resulted in fluorescence quenching GQDs by gold NPs. It was noticed that the existence of Pb2+ can trigger the DNAzyme and cut the DNA linkers thus leading to separation of AuNPs and GQDs. Fluorescence of GQDs is recovered this way, and the quantitative study of Pb2+ was made possible (Niu et al., 2018b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the conventional sensors and nanosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional optical sensors like microsensors, liquid chromatography, X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy, atomic absorption, anodic stripping voltammetry, inductively coupled-optical emission spectroscopy, inductively coupled-mass spectrometry, capillary electrophoresis and UV–Visible spectroscopy are largely used in biology, environmental science, marine science, biotechnology and clinical medicine for a past few years (Wang, 2007). Despite their use for such a long period, they still have many limitations such as sample preparation, long-time response time, cleanup, expensive instrument, unsuitability for microfluids, preconcentration, and restriction of action on the surface of their dimension (Buledi et al., 2021). These limitations paved the way for nanomaterial-based sensors or nanosensors. High selectivity, portability, user acceptance, quick treatment, least sample pre-treatment and large sensitivity of nanosensors have made them a great candidate to replace those conventional sensors (Numan et al., 2021). Table 2 represents a comparison between conventional and nanomaterials-based sensors based on their limits of detection.

Rapid urbanization and globalization made a selective yet sensitive identification of heavy and toxic metal ions due to high demand to ensure human and environmental safety. The search through literature revealed that the nanomaterials based sensors have boosted the heavy metal ion detection mainly because of their excellent properties like competent fluorescence quenching, bright fluorescence, quick response time, very small detection limit (can detect even minute amount of metal), portable (user friendly, and cost-effective), high resolution (sensitivity towards the signal produced a concentration of analyte), selectivity towards one metal in the presence of other metals and increased surface area to charge different species. These advantages of nanomaterials-based sensors over the conventional sensors have made them favorable methods for identifying heavy metal ions. Nanosensors have replaced many traditional techniques due to their low cost and less time consumption. This review has highlighted the existing information for optical heavy metal ions sensing by using different nano architectures. Comprehensive information has been given to define different sorts of nanomaterial architectures for heavy metal ions recognition. This is a mutual scientific concern that Metal NPs, CNTs, Quantum Dots, Luminescent MOFs and Graphene are some of the most suitable substrates to use as efficient fluorescence quenchers. These quenchers have broadly been consumed for constructing platforms for metal ion sensing. It was long noted that nano-architectures frequently serve as effective fluorescence probes to directly (or by modification with some large or small biomolecules) sense heavy metal ions for improved selectivity. There are still some challenges to manufacturing nanosensors with high performance. These challenges include doping of expensive receptor on the nanomaterial-based sensor, applying appropriate sample preparation as nanosensors are extremely surface active and may react with other species present, nanosensors are calibrated with few standards, which decrease the reliability of the result, and nanotoxicity may arise when nanosensors are used by common people and are released in the environment as such. After a detailed review it could be suggested that most of the stress in research arena should be given to mature exceedingly specific sensors under strict environmental conditions for determining the heavy metals. The first concern of researchers should be the formation of an efficient, unique and cost-effective combination of nanomaterials to detect metal ions and other contaminants. Furthermore, the simultaneous designing of multiple metal ion-based detection system needs great attention for the upcoming progress of this research topic, exclusively in colorimetric or optical fluorescence nanosensors for heavy metal cations. The selectivity of the nanosensors should be enhanced to avoid interference by the presence of other metal ions. The researchers should also focus on the nanosensors, which will give reliable results after calibration with many standards. The formation of nontoxic nanosensors is the demand of the day. It will decrease the pollution caused by the use of nano-toxic nanosensors. This review has discussed the potential advantages of nanosensors over other sensors and the limitations of nanosensors that are needed to overcome to make them efficient sensing tools in the laboratories and outside the laboratories. Researchers and scientists are working in the right direction to synthesize nanosensors not only for heavy metal ions but also for other contaminants, macromolecules and biomolecules. The time demands to minimize the shortcomings and maximize the efficiency. The progress in nanosensors will bring a positive change in the field of environmental safety, food industry, health science, biomedicine and agriculture.
